name: Prose Style Pattern Watchlist
purpose: A machine-readable watchlist of generic prose patterns for Prose Cleanup Mode. These are patterns to check for OVERUSE, not banned constructions. None of them prove AI authorship, and each has legitimate human uses. Reduce them when they weaken a specific sentence; keep them when they serve the writing.
safety_note: This is not an AI detector and not proof of AI authorship. Do not use it to evade detection. Improve clarity and accountability, never deception. Preserve meaning and facts; never fabricate specifics to 'fix' a pattern.

sources.title: Stop Slop
sources.author: Hardik Pandya
sources.adaptation_note: Pattern ideas adapted and reworded with safer, non-absolutist framing and false-positive notes.

patterns.why_generic: Announces that a point is coming instead of making it, delaying the reader.
patterns.safer_alternative: Delete the opener and lead with the actual claim.
patterns.false_positive_note: Conversational and spoken-style pieces sometimes use these deliberately for voice.

patterns.why_generic: Tells the reader something is important rather than showing why.
patterns.safer_alternative: Cut the crutch; let the substance carry the emphasis.
patterns.false_positive_note: Rhetorical and persuasive writing may use one sparingly for genuine effect.

patterns.why_generic: Obscures plain meaning behind interchangeable corporate vocabulary.
patterns.false_positive_note: Some terms are precise in their native domain (e.g., 'leverage' in finance).

patterns.why_generic: Asserts importance without naming the specific thing that is important.
patterns.safer_alternative: Name the specific reason, implication, or stake — with a source if it is a factual claim.
patterns.false_positive_note: A summary line can be acceptable when the specifics are given right after.

patterns.why_generic: Adds emphasis without adding meaning; dilutes the sentence.
patterns.safer_alternative: Reduce overuse; keep an adverb only where it changes the meaning.
patterns.false_positive_note: Adverbs are normal, useful words. This is about overuse, not elimination — do not strip every -ly word.

examples: the rest of this essay explains
patterns.why_generic: Narrates the structure instead of letting the writing move.
patterns.safer_alternative: Delete the aside and proceed directly to the content.
patterns.false_positive_note: Textbooks, tutorials, and academic abstracts legitimately signpost structure.

examples: the answer isn't X, it's Y
patterns.why_generic: A telegraphed reversal that adds drama but not information.
patterns.safer_alternative: State Y directly and drop the negation.
patterns.false_positive_note: A genuine contrast that corrects a real misconception is fine.

examples: not a X. not a Y. a Z.
it wasn't X. it wasn't Y. it was Z.
patterns.why_generic: Builds suspense through negation before stating the point.
patterns.safer_alternative: State what it is; skip the runway of what it is not.
patterns.false_positive_note: Occasionally effective in narrative or speech writing.

patterns.why_generic: Sentence fragments stacked for manufactured profundity.
patterns.safer_alternative: Use complete sentences; vary length so emphasis lands naturally.
patterns.false_positive_note: Sparing fragments are a legitimate stylistic device.

patterns.why_generic: Announces insight or grants permission instead of delivering the point.
patterns.safer_alternative: Make the point and let readers draw their own conclusion.
patterns.false_positive_note: A single rhetorical question can work in persuasive or teaching contexts.

patterns.why_generic: Gives abstractions human verbs, hiding who actually acted.
patterns.safer_alternative: Name the person or group who did it, or use 'you' to put the reader in the seat.
patterns.false_positive_note: Some idioms ('the data shows') are accepted shorthand in technical writing.

patterns.why_generic: Floats above the subject instead of grounding it in concrete actors or examples.
patterns.safer_alternative: Put the reader in the scene or cite a specific case.
patterns.false_positive_note: Generalization is appropriate in genuinely general claims, when sourced.

patterns.why_generic: Passive voice that conceals who did something, draining accountability.
patterns.safer_alternative: Name the actor and put them at the front of the sentence.
patterns.false_positive_note: Passive voice is correct when the actor is unknown, irrelevant, or the object is the real topic (common in science writing).
